quiz_question: Which medieval polymath designed a calendar still in use nearly a thousand years later?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, as an astronomer, Omar Khayyam calculated the duration of the solar year with remarkable precision and designed the Jalali calendar — a solar calendar with a very precise 33-year intercalation cycle. This calendar provided the basis for the Persian calendar that is still in use after nearly a millennium.

quiz_question: What kind of equations did Khayyam first solve generally, centuries before European mathematicians?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Omar Khayyam was the first to provide a general solution for all third-degree (cubic) equations by using the intersection of two conic sections — a method often later attributed to Descartes. He performed these geometric calculations by selecting a unit length while strictly adhering to the rule of homogeneity.

quiz_question: Which 9th-century treatise's title gave us the modern word 'algebra'?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the English term 'algebra' comes from the short-hand title of al-Khwarizmi's treatise 'Al-Jabr' (The Compendious Book on Calculation by Completion and Balancing), compiled between 813 and 833. It presented the first systematic solution of linear and quadratic equations and defined algebra as an independent discipline.

quiz_question: Whose Latinized name became the modern word 'algorithm'?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, al-Khwarizmi's name gave rise to the English terms 'algorism' and 'algorithm'. When his mathematical texts were translated into Latin in the 12th century, his name was Latinized as 'Algoritmi' — and this became the root of 'algorithm', the word now used for every computer procedure in the world.

quiz_question: Whose 9th-century textbook introduced the decimal positional number system to the Western world?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in the 12th century, Latin translations of al-Khwarizmi's textbook on Indian arithmetic introduced the decimal-based positional number system to the Western world. This codified the Indian numerals (0-9) that we use in all modern mathematics and everyday counting.

quiz_question: How many unsolved problems did David Hilbert famously present in 1900 to guide 20th-century math?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in 1900 David Hilbert presented a collection of problems that set a course for mathematical research of the 20th century. These 23 problems became one of the most influential challenges in the history of mathematics, and several remain unsolved to this day.

quiz_question: What branch of mathematics did David Hilbert co-found, aimed at proving all math from first principles?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, David Hilbert was a co-founder of proof theory and mathematical logic. He and his students also developed important tools used in modern mathematical physics. Hilbert spaces — infinite-dimensional vector spaces — are now fundamental to quantum mechanics.

quiz_question: How do most waterfalls form and grow taller over time?
correct_answer: Soft rock erodes faster below hard rock
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the most common method of waterfall formation is that a river courses over a top layer of resistant bedrock before falling onto softer rock, which erodes faster. This differential erosion causes an increasingly high fall to develop over geological time.

quiz_question: Who won the very first Nobel Prize in Physics in 1901?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in 1901 Wilhelm Rontgen became the first recipient of the Nobel Prize in Physics 'in recognition of the extraordinary services he has rendered by the discovery of the remarkable rays subsequently named after him.' The element roentgenium is also named in his honor.

quiz_question: Which physicist coined the term 'quantum entanglement' and devised the Schrodinger's cat thought experiment?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Schrodinger coined the term 'quantum entanglement' in 1935 and is also known for his Schrodinger equation and Schrodinger's cat thought experiment. He shared the 1933 Nobel Prize in Physics with Paul Dirac.

quiz_question: Which element became the first predominantly artificial element produced, in 1937?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in 1937 technetium became the first predominantly artificial element to be produced, hence its name (from the Greek technetos, 'artificial'). It has atomic number 43 and is the lightest element whose isotopes are all radioactive.

quiz_question: What did Mendeleev call the element that would later be named technetium?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, many of technetium's properties had been predicted by Dmitri Mendeleev before it was discovered. Mendeleev noted a gap in his periodic table and gave the undiscovered element the provisional name 'ekamanganese' (Em).

quiz_question: What maximum wind speed can the most extreme tornadoes reach?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, while most tornadoes have wind speeds less than 180 km/h, the most extreme tornadoes can attain wind speeds of more than 480 kilometres per hour (300 mph). They can also be more than 3 kilometres in diameter and travel over 100 km.

quiz_question: Which country experiences far more tornadoes than any other nation on Earth?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, tornadoes most commonly occur in North America, particularly in central and southeastern regions of the United States known as Tornado Alley. The United States has by far the most tornadoes of any country in the world.

quiz_question: What was Antonie van Leeuwenhoek's actual profession before becoming the 'Father of Microbiology'?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, van Leeuwenhoek worked as a draper (cloth merchant) in his youth and founded his own draper shop in 1654. A largely self-taught man in science, he developed an interest in lensmaking and became the first to observe bacteria, spermatozoa, and red blood cells through microscopes he built himself.

quiz_question: Which of these did Leeuwenhoek NOT observe first through a microscope?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Leeuwenhoek was the first to document microscopic observations of muscle fibers, bacteria, spermatozoa, red blood cells, and was among the first to see blood flow in capillaries. Cell division was documented by other scientists.

quiz_question: How many years after Wegener proposed continental drift was his hypothesis finally accepted?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Wegener proposed the continental drift hypothesis in 1912, but it was not accepted by mainstream geology until the 1950s, when palaeomagnetic discoveries provided strong support. That is roughly 40 years of rejection before he was vindicated.

quiz_question: Where did Alfred Wegener die while conducting polar research expeditions?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, Wegener died in Greenland in November 1930 during an expedition. He was involved in several expeditions to Greenland to study polar air circulation, and his expedition teams were the first to overwinter on the inland Greenland ice sheet.

quiz_question: What material was the original official kilogram cylinder made of?
explanation: According to Wikipedia, in 1889 a cylinder composed of platinum-iridium — the International Prototype of the Kilogram (IPK) — became the world's mass standard and remained so for 130 years. Every mass measurement on Earth was ultimately traceable to this one object in France.

quiz_question: What substance was used to define the kilogram when first created in 1795?
distractors: The mass of a mole of hydrogen
explanation: According to Wikipedia, the kilogram was originally defined during the French Revolution as the mass of one litre of water at approximately 4 degrees C (its maximum density). The current Planck-constant definition still agrees with this original to within 30 parts per million (0.003%).
